required.title: [Book] The Complete Cookbook for Young Chefs
required.author: America's Test Kitchen Kids

required.title: [Book] Cooking Class: 57 Fun Recipes Kids Will Love to Make (and Eat!)
required.author: Deanna F. Cook

suggested.title: [Book] Kid Chef: The Foodie Kids Cookbook
suggested.author: Melina Hammer

suggested.title: [Read Aloud] Bread Comes to Life: A Garden of Wheat and a Loaf to Eat
suggested.author: George Levenson

supplementary.title: [Activity] Grow an herb on the windowsill

competence_markers.description: The student can read a recipe fully before cooking and identify all needed ingredients and equipment
The student can cook basic foods on a stovetop with appropriate safety (scrambled eggs, grilled cheese, pasta, simple sauteed vegetables) — supervised but not directed
The student can use an oven safely (with supervision): preheating, setting timers, using oven mitts, placing and removing items
The student can explain the three components of a balanced meal and assemble meals that include all three
The student has a regular weekly cooking responsibility and meets it consistently — on time, complete, with cleanup
The student can prepare at least six different meals from recipes with minimal assistance
The student demonstrates consistent kitchen safety habits without reminders
The student plans before cooking — reads the full recipe, checks ingredients, and organizes their workspace before turning on the stove

cross_connections.description: Measuring, doubling recipes, fractions (half a cup, quarter teaspoon) — cooking is daily math practice.
Why does bread rise? What happens to an egg when you heat it? Why does salt change the taste? Cooking is applied science with immediate, edible results.
Holding a weekly meal responsibility teaches the discipline of consistent delivery — cooking on schedule whether you feel like it or not.
Kitchen cleanup after cooking is both a cooking habit and a cleaning competence.
Understanding heat as both a tool and a hazard — the same principle applies at the campfire and at the stove.

content.why_this_matters:
You already know where food comes from. You already know how to be safe in the kitchen. Now it is time to take the next step: making real meals, start to finish, on your own.

When you can walk into a kitchen and make yourself breakfast without asking anyone for help, something changes. You are no longer someone who waits to be fed. You are someone who feeds yourself. That is a real kind of independence — the kind that stays with you forever.

This packet pushes you further. You will learn to follow recipes, use basic cooking techniques with heat, plan what you need before you start, and prepare meals that are not just edible but good. You will also begin to understand what makes a meal balanced — not because someone hands you a chart, but because you learn what your body actually needs and how food provides it.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Recipe following** — reading a recipe, gathering ingredients, following steps in order, adjusting when something goes wrong
2. **Basic heat cooking** — using a stovetop and oven with increasing independence (supervised)
3. **Meal completeness** — understanding that a meal has parts (protein, vegetable, grain/starch) and assembling complete meals
4. **Planning before cooking** — reading the recipe fully, checking that all ingredients are available, understanding timing
5. **Expanding the repertoire** — moving beyond the three basic meals of A1 to a broader range of foods you can prepare
6. **Kitchen ownership** — taking responsibility for meals on a regular schedule, not as an occasional project
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Reading a Recipe

A recipe is a set of instructions. Like building instructions for a LEGO set, a recipe tells you what you need and what to do, in what order. Learning to read a recipe is a skill — and like any skill, it improves with practice.

**Before you start cooking, always do these things:**

1. **Read the entire recipe first.** All of it. Start to finish. Do not start cooking after reading the first step. You need to know what is coming — including how long things take, what order things happen in, and whether you need to do something ahead of time (like preheat the oven or let butter soften).

2. **Check the ingredients.** Do you have everything the recipe calls for? In the right amounts? There is nothing more frustrating than getting halfway through a recipe and discovering you are missing an ingredient.

3. **Check the equipment.** Do you have the right pots, pans, bowls, and utensils? If the recipe calls for a 9x13 baking pan and you do not have one, find out before you have mixed the batter.

4. **Understand the terms.** If a recipe says "dice," "fold," "simmer," or "cream the butter," do you know what those mean? If not, look them up or ask your mentor before you start. Cooking has its own vocabulary.

### Part 2: Working with Heat

Heat transforms food. It changes texture, flavor, color, and safety. A raw egg is liquid; a cooked egg is solid. Raw chicken can make you sick; cooked chicken is safe and delicious. Understanding heat is the difference between someone who assembles food and someone who cooks.

**Stovetop basics:**
- Burners have settings from low to high. Low heat simmers and warms. Medium heat cooks most things. High heat boils water and sears meat.
- A pan needs to be warm before food goes in. Cold pan = food sticks.
- Stir or flip food to cook evenly. Food sitting still on one side burns on the bottom while the top stays raw.
- When something is sizzling, it is cooking. When it is smoking, the heat is too high. Turn it down.
- Handle hot pans with oven mitts or dry towels. Never with wet cloth — steam burns are severe.

**Oven basics:**
- Always preheat. Putting food in a cold oven changes cooking times and results.
- Use the middle rack unless the recipe says otherwise.
- Set a timer. Do not guess.
- Open the oven door as little as possible — every time you open it, heat escapes and the temperature drops.

**You will practice stovetop and oven cooking with your mentor present.** As you demonstrate consistent safety habits, your mentor will gradually step back. The goal is not unsupervised cooking at this age — it is building the technique and awareness that will make independence safe when you are ready.

### Part 3: What Makes a Meal

A meal is not just one food. A good meal has parts that work together:

- **Protein** — the part that builds and repairs your body. Eggs, chicken, beef, fish, beans, lentils, cheese, nuts.
- **Vegetables or fruit** — the part that gives you vitamins, minerals, and fiber. Broccoli, carrots, salad, peppers, apples, berries.
- **Grain or starch** — the part that gives you energy. Rice, bread, pasta, potatoes, oatmeal.

When you plan a meal, think about these three parts. A plate of just pasta is not a meal — it is a plate of starch. Add chicken and broccoli, and now it is a meal. A bowl of fruit is a snack. Add yogurt and granola, and it is closer to a meal.

You do not need to be perfect about this. But start noticing: does my plate have all three parts? Over time, this becomes automatic — you see what is missing and add it without thinking.

### Part 4: Meals You Will Learn to Make

Build your skills through these categories, moving from simpler to more complex:

**Breakfast mastery:**
- Scrambled eggs (stovetop — controlling heat so they are creamy, not rubbery)
- Pancakes from scratch (measuring, mixing, managing the griddle)
- Oatmeal with toppings (stovetop or microwave, with fruit, nuts, and honey)
- Toast with avocado or egg (combining techniques)

**Lunch mastery:**
- Grilled cheese sandwich (stovetop — managing heat for golden bread and melted cheese)
- Pasta with butter and vegetables (boiling water, timing pasta, sauteing vegetables)
- Soup from a recipe (following a multi-step recipe with stovetop cooking)
- Wraps and roll-ups (assembly with multiple ingredients — protein, vegetables, sauce)

**Dinner contributions:**
- Salad with homemade dressing (whisking oil, vinegar, salt, pepper, herbs)
- A side dish (roasted vegetables in the oven, steamed rice, baked potatoes)
- Full participation in a family dinner preparation — assigned a complete component to prepare independently

**Baking introduction:**
- Muffins (measuring precisely, oven use, timing)
- Simple cookies (following a recipe exactly — baking punishes imprecision more than stovetop cooking)
- Quick bread (banana bread or zucchini bread — a forgiving introduction to baking)

### Part 5: Planning and Responsibility

Cooking is not just what happens at the stove. It is what happens before: deciding what to make, checking that you have what you need, and managing your time so the meal is ready when it should be.

**Weekly cooking assignment:** By the midpoint of this packet, you should have a regular cooking responsibility — at least one meal per week that is yours to plan and prepare. Your mentor approves the plan, but you do the work: choose the recipe, verify ingredients, prepare the food, serve it, and clean up.

This is not a project. It is a responsibility. The household is counting on you for that meal. It needs to be ready on time, it needs to be edible, and the kitchen needs to be clean when you are done.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Recipe Reading
**Developing well:** The child reads the entire recipe before starting, gathers all ingredients and equipment first, and follows steps in order. They catch potential problems before they start — "We don't have enough eggs for this."

**Developing slowly:** The child starts cooking after reading the first step and discovers missing ingredients mid-recipe. They skip steps or get confused by the sequence.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you modeling recipe reading yourself? Cook something together and narrate your own process: "First I read the whole thing. Then I check — do we have everything?"

### 2. Heat Competence
**Developing well:** The child adjusts heat appropriately — turns it down when something is browning too fast, waits for the pan to heat before adding food. They use oven mitts automatically and handle hot items with care and awareness.

**Developing slowly:** The child defaults to high heat for everything, does not notice burning until smoke appears, or is afraid of the stove and avoids it.

**Diagnostic questions:** For timid children — have you done enough stovetop sessions? Confidence comes from repetition. For reckless children — have you had the direct conversation about burns and their consequences? Sometimes a frank discussion about what a burn feels like and how long it takes to heal is what makes the hazard real.

### 3. Meal Completeness
**Developing well:** The child naturally plans meals with multiple components. They think about what goes with what. They understand that a plate of bread is not a meal.

**Developing slowly:** Every meal the child plans is a single item — pasta, or eggs, or a sandwich. They do not think in terms of components.

**Diagnostic questions:** Point to meals you eat together and name the components. "See — there is the protein, there is the vegetable, there is the starch. When you plan your meal, what will your three parts be?" The framework is simple. It just needs to be named explicitly.

### 4. Weekly Responsibility
**Developing well:** The child plans and executes their weekly meal without reminders. They take it seriously. The meal is ready on time and the kitchen is clean.

**Developing slowly:** You remind them every week. They scramble last-minute. The kitchen is a mess afterward. The meal is incomplete or late.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the expectation clear and non-negotiable? If the child perceives the weekly meal as optional, they will treat it as optional. Set the day, set the time, and hold the standard. If they forget, the natural consequence is that the household does not have that meal prepared — make the gap visible rather than silently covering for them.
